steps.title: Welcome to Day 19: AI for Finance & HR
steps.body:
Finance and HR are data-intensive, process-heavy domains that run on accuracy and compliance. Every mistake costs money or trust. That makes them ideal candidates for AI — not to replace the experts, but to eliminate the manual drudgery that distracts them from strategic work.

Today you'll learn how to:
• Automate financial analysis and reporting
• Build budgets with AI-powered forecasting
• Streamline HR processes from hiring to onboarding
• Screen and rank candidates based on objective criteria
• Create onboarding flows that don't require hours of admin work

Finance and HR are the backbone of every organization — and AI makes that backbone stronger.

AI in Finance & HR — the high-impact use cases:

💰 FINANCIAL ANALYSIS — AI ingests transaction data, identifies spending patterns, flags anomalies, and generates monthly reports. Your data never sleeps.

📊 BUDGETING & FORECASTING — AI analyzes historical trends, seasonality, and external factors to project future revenue, expenses, and cash flow. Forecasts update in real time.

📋 HR PROCESS AUTOMATION — AI handles policy Q&A, leave requests, benefits enrollment assistance, and employee data management. HR becomes self-service for employees.

🔍 RECRUITMENT — AI screens resumes, ranks candidates by job fit, drafts interview questions, and identifies potential bias in job descriptions. Fairer hiring, faster.

🚀 ONBOARDING — AI generates personalized onboarding plans, schedules training, answers new hire questions, and tracks completion. New employees productive faster.

steps.title: Elena's AI Finance & HR Transformation
steps.body:
Elena is head of operations at a 100-person company. She owns finance and HR for the org. Before AI, she was drowning:

BEFORE AI:
• Monthly financial report: 3 days of exporting, reconciling, formatting, analyzing
• Budgeting season: 2 weeks of spreadsheet chaos with department heads
• Resume screening for a new hire: 8 hours to get 40 resumes down to 5 candidates
• New hire onboarding: 4 hours of manual paperwork, IT setup, and scheduling per person

AFTER AI:

💰 FINANCIAL REPORTING: AI connects to bank feeds, categorizes transactions, flags anomalies, and generates an executive summary each Monday. Elena reviews for 30 minutes instead of building for 3 days.

📊 BUDGETING: AI takes historical data + department growth plans and generates a first-pass budget with scenario modeling. 'What if we cut travel by 20% and add 2 headcount in engineering?' — AI recalculates in seconds.

🔍 RECRUITMENT: Job posting goes up. 200 applications arrive. AI screens all 200 against the job requirements, ranks them, and highlights the top 15. Elena reviews the top candidates' full profiles — she reads 15 instead of 200.

🚀 ONBOARDING: New hire accepts offer. AI generates: Day 1 schedule, required forms pre-filled, IT access list, training modules, team intro emails, 30-60-90 day plan. Elena reviews for 15 minutes instead of building for 4 hours.

RESULT: Elena reclaimed 25 hours per month. More importantly, her reports are more accurate, her hires are more qualified, and new employees are productive faster.

steps.question: When using AI for resume screening, what's the most important thing to set up correctly?
options: The file format of the resumes
Objective, bias-checked screening criteria that focus on job-relevant skills and experience
The number of candidates you want the AI to flag
Making sure the AI uses the latest resume format standards
feedback: Exactly. If your screening criteria are biased (e.g., 'top university only' or '5+ years of a specific tool'), AI will replicate and amplify that bias at scale. Define criteria that measure actual job readiness: relevant skills, demonstrated impact, problem-solving ability. Review the AI's screening decisions regularly for bias patterns.

Not quite. The most important thing is the screening criteria themselves. Bad criteria produce bad rankings — even if the AI runs perfectly. Define skills and experience that actually predict job success, and audit the AI's decisions for hidden bias.

steps.question: AI-generated financial reports should be considered final and can be shared with stakeholders without human review.
feedback: Correct. AI financial reports are drafts. They can miss context (one-time charges, accounting policy changes), misclassify transactions, or produce numbers that 'look right' but are wrong. Always review: check totals against source data, verify unusual line items, and add your context and commentary.

Not true. AI-generated financial reports need human review before they leave finance. AI can misclassify transactions, miss context, or generate convincing-but-wrong numbers. The finance professional's judgment — and accountability — is what makes a report trustworthy.

steps.question: In financial analysis, using AI to detect transactions that don't match expected patterns — which could indicate errors, fraud, or unusual activity — is called ___ detection.
feedback: Correct. Anomaly detection is one of AI's strongest finance applications. AI learns normal transaction patterns and flags anything that deviates. It catches things humans miss — especially in high-volume environments where manual review would miss subtle patterns.
Good answer. The term is anomaly detection — using AI to identify transactions or patterns that fall outside normal parameters. It's a powerful early warning system for both errors and fraud.

steps.title: AI-Powered Onboarding Flow
steps.body:
HERE'S WHAT A FULLY AI-POWERED ONBOARDING LOOKS LIKE:

🌟 DAY -7 (BEFORE START):
• AI generates personalized welcome email with team info, first day agenda, what to bring
• AI generates IT setup ticket: laptop specs, software access, permissions, security training
• AI pre-fills employment forms with info from offer letter
• AI schedules: Day 1 intro meetings, 30-60-90 day plan, first week training sessions

🌟 DAY 1:
• New hire arrives. AI-powered HR assistant answers their questions: 'How do I set up my benefits?' 'Where's the office kitchen?' 'Who do I report to?'
• AI sends team introduction email with new hire's background and role

🌟 WEEK 1:
• AI guides new hire through training modules, checks completion, sends gentle reminders
• AI schedules 15-minute check-ins with key team members (spread across the week, not all Day 1)

🌟 DAY 30:
• AI generates a first-month progress summary: training completed, connections made, early wins
• AI schedules manager review meeting and provides suggested discussion topics

🌟 DAY 90:
• AI compiles full onboarding assessment: completed training, feedback scores, ramp-up metrics
• New hire is now fully operational. The system hands off to ongoing management.

Every step is automated. The HR team only steps in for exceptions and personal touches. New hires feel supported, not lost.

steps.question: Using AI to screen job descriptions for biased language (gender-coded words, age assumptions) and suggest more inclusive alternatives.
feedback: This is an excellent use case. AI is great at pattern matching against known bias indicators — 'ninja,' 'rockstar,' 'young and energetic,' etc. — and suggesting neutral alternatives. It's not perfect (context matters), but it catches bias that most human writers don't notice. Run every job description through this before posting.

Absolutely. AI bias detection in job descriptions is a proven application. Tools can flag gendered language, unnecessary requirements, and subtle assumptions. Use it as a first pass, then have a human review for context-specific adjustments.

steps.question: Deciding whether to give a specific employee a promotion, including factors they won't put in writing.
feedback: Right. Promotion decisions involve factors beyond data: team dynamics, growth trajectory, cultural impact, and sometimes context that isn't documented. AI can help by compiling performance data, peer feedback, and skills growth metrics — but the final promotion decision is a human leadership judgment.

Not ideal for full automation. AI can compile objective data (performance reviews, skills growth, project outcomes) to inform the decision. But promotion decisions involve leadership judgment about potential, team fit, and future trajectory that data alone can't capture.

fields.label: Which domain are you automating — Finance or HR?
fields.placeholder: e.g., Finance reporting, Recruitment, Onboarding, Budgeting

fields.label: Describe your current manual process
fields.placeholder: e.g., I manually copy data from bank feeds into a spreadsheet and build a report each month

fields.label: What data sources does this process use?
fields.placeholder: e.g., Bank accounts, payroll system, expense reports, CRM

fields.label: What compliance or accuracy requirements exist?
fields.placeholder: e.g., Must be audit-ready, must pass SOX compliance, must preserve data privacy

steps.template:
FINANCE/HR AUTOMATION DESIGN

DOMAIN: {domain}

CURRENT PROCESS:
{current_process}

DATA SOURCES:
{data_sources}

COMPLIANCE REQUIREMENTS:
{compliance_needs}

AI WORKFLOW:
1. AI extracts data from all sources
2. AI classifies, reconciles, and flags anomalies
3. AI generates first draft of output (report, ranking, plan)
4. Human reviews, adds context, approves
5. Final output is delivered or acted upon

HUMAN OVERSIGHT IS REQUIRED BEFORE FINAL APPROVAL.

steps.body:
FINANCE / HR AI SYSTEM PROMPT

"You are an AI process automation consultant specializing in [FINANCE / HR].

Current process: [DESCRIBE]

Data available: [LIST]

Compliance concerns: [LIST]

Analyze this process and design an AI automation that:

1. Maps the current manual steps
2. Identifies which steps AI can handle (and which require human judgment)
3. Specifies the data flow between systems
4. Lists 5-10 edge cases and how each should be handled
5. Recommends a human oversight cadence (daily reviews? weekly audits?)
6. Estimates time savings and risk level

Be specific about the AI/human split. Compliance is non-negotiable."

Think of a finance or HR task you deal with — budgeting, expense tracking, hiring, onboarding, benefits Q&A. Describe it briefly, then outline how you'd automate it with AI. What would AI do vs. what would you do?

steps.prompt:
Finance and HR are ultimately about trust — trust in numbers, trust in people, trust in processes.

• How would AI change the level of trust in your organization's financial or HR processes?
• What's the biggest risk of over-automating in these domains?
• Where would AI add the most value to your team's operations?

questions.label: What excites you most about AI in Finance/HR?
options: Eliminating manual data entry and reporting drudgery
Real-time financial insights without the wait
Self-service HR that works for employees

questions.label: What finance or HR process would you love to automate but haven't yet?
questions.placeholder: Describe the process and what's holding you back...

steps.title: Day 19 Complete! 💰
steps.body:
You now understand how AI transforms finance and HR — from financial analysis and budgeting to recruitment screening and employee onboarding.

Key takeaways:
• Financial reports need human review — AI drafts, finance pros validate
• AI forecasting uses historical data + external factors for real-time projections
• Resume screening works best with bias-checked, skill-focused criteria
• Onboarding automation creates consistent, personalized experiences
• Compliance and accuracy are non-negotiable — AI handles volume, humans own accountability

Tomorrow: AI for Creative Work — design, content, video, audio, and AI-assisted creativity.
